passages.explanation: The Lord appoints seventy-two others and sends them out two by two ahead of him to every town and place where he's about to go. He tells them: the harvest is plentiful, but the workers are few — ask the Lord of the harvest to send out workers. Go — I'm sending you out like lambs among wolves. Don't take a purse, bag, or sandals. Don't greet anyone on the road. When you enter a house, first say 'peace to this house.' If someone who promotes peace is there, your peace will rest on them. Stay in that house, eating and drinking what they give you — the worker deserves his wages. Don't move from house to house. When you enter a town and are welcomed, eat what's offered, heal the sick, and tell them the kingdom of God has come near. But when you're not welcomed, go into the streets and say: even the dust of your town that sticks to our feet we wipe off against you — yet be sure of this: the kingdom of God has come near. It will be more bearable for Sodom on that day than for that town. The seventy-two represent a wider mission than the Twelve — some scholars link the number to the seventy nations listed in Genesis 10, hinting at a universal scope.

Jesus pronounces woe on the cities that have seen the most miracles but haven't repented. Woe to you, Chorazin! Woe to you, Bethsaida! If the miracles performed in you had been performed in Tyre and Sidon, they would have repented long ago, sitting in sackcloth and ashes. And you, Capernaum — will you be lifted to the heavens? No, you'll go down to Hades. Whoever listens to you listens to me; whoever rejects you rejects me; whoever rejects me rejects the one who sent me. Greater privilege means greater accountability. The Galilean towns that saw Jesus daily are judged more severely than pagan cities that never had the chance.

The seventy-two return with joy: Lord, even the demons submit to us in your name! Jesus replies: I saw Satan fall like lightning from heaven. I've given you authority to trample on snakes and scorpions and to overcome all the power of the enemy — nothing will harm you. However, don't rejoice that the spirits submit to you, but rejoice that your names are written in heaven. At that time, full of joy through the Holy Spirit, Jesus says: I praise you, Father, Lord of heaven and earth, because you've hidden these things from the wise and learned, and revealed them to little children. Yes, Father, for this is what you were pleased to do. All things have been committed to me by my Father. No one knows who the Son is except the Father, and no one knows who the Father is except the Son and those to whom the Son chooses to reveal him. Then he turns to his disciples privately: blessed are the eyes that see what you see. Many prophets and kings wanted to see what you see but didn't, and to hear what you hear but didn't. Jesus redirects their excitement — your identity matters more than your power.

An expert in the law stands up to test Jesus: teacher, what must I do to inherit eternal life? Jesus asks: what's written in the law? How do you read it? The man answers: love the Lord your God with all your heart, soul, strength, and mind, and love your neighbour as yourself. Jesus says: you've answered correctly — do this and you will live. But the man, wanting to justify himself, asks: and who is my neighbour? Jesus tells the parable of the Good Samaritan. A man going from Jerusalem to Jericho is attacked by robbers, stripped, beaten, and left half dead. A priest sees him and passes by on the other side. A Levite does the same. But a Samaritan — someone Jews despised — sees the man, has compassion, bandages his wounds, puts him on his own donkey, brings him to an inn, and takes care of him. The next day he gives the innkeeper two denarii and says: look after him, and when I return I'll reimburse you for any extra expense. Jesus asks: which of the three was a neighbour to the man who fell into the hands of robbers? The expert can't even say the word 'Samaritan' — he answers: the one who had mercy. Jesus says: go and do likewise. The question was 'who is my neighbour?' — Jesus reframes it: the real question is 'are you being a neighbour?'

Jesus and his disciples come to a village where a woman named Martha opens her home to him. Her sister Mary sits at the Lord's feet, listening to what he says. Martha is distracted by all the preparations. She comes to Jesus: Lord, don't you care that my sister has left me to do the work by myself? Tell her to help me! The Lord answers: Martha, Martha, you are worried and upset about many things, but few things are needed — or indeed only one. Mary has chosen what is better, and it will not be taken from her. Jesus isn't condemning hospitality or service — he's saying that in this moment, listening to him is the priority. Mary sitting at a rabbi's feet as a student is itself remarkable for a woman in that culture.
